title: Expressing support for honoring Earth Day, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This resolution supports celebrating Earth Day, which started in 1970 and helped create important environmental laws and the Environmental Protection Agency. The House wants Americans to join Earth Day activities and learn about protecting the environment. The resolution also asks the government to rejoin the Paris Agreement, which is a deal between countries to fight climate change. It urges leaders to take quick action to solve climate problems and fix environmental issues that hurt certain communities unfairly.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This is a resolution that shows support for honoring Earth Day and other related purposes.

sections.title: Background on Earth Day
sections.text: Earth Day was first celebrated on April twenty-second, nineteen seventy. Senator Gaylord Nelson, who started Earth Day, asked Americans to hold their elected officials responsible for not doing enough to protect people's health and the natural environment. After seeing years of environmental problems being ignored, millions of Americans from all backgrounds came together in cities across the country on the first Earth Day. They demanded basic protections for our planet and promised to leave behind a healthier world for future generations. Earth Day brought together people who had been fighting against oil spills, polluting factories and power plants, raw sewage, toxic dumps, loss of wildlife, and other environmental problems. This work continues today. Earth Day is a yearly event created to promote environmental citizenship and action throughout the year. The first Earth Day celebration was the largest environmental grassroots event ever held at that time and is widely credited with starting the modern environmental movement.

sections.title: Earth Day Impact and Growth
sections.text: The voices of the first Earth Day participants led to an unmatched period of legislative and grassroots activity that changed how we interact with the environment. This led to the creation of the Environmental Protection Agency and the passage of laws such as the Federal Water Pollution Control Act, the Clean Air Act, and the Endangered Species Act of nineteen seventy-three. By nineteen ninety, Earth Day was celebrated around the world, with two hundred million people in one hundred forty-one countries participating in Earth Day events. Earth Day encourages environmental activism and stewardship around the world and reminds people to find ways to protect the environment, including managing their own environmental impact. Earth Day serves as an opportunity to learn from and build community with indigenous communities, who have taken care of the land for generations and have great wisdom on preserving biodiversity.

sections.title: Earth Day Activities and Challenges
sections.text: On Earth Day, people engage in projects, initiatives, and campaigns to protect and restore the planet including planting trees, removing litter, cleaning up coral reefs, conserving water and energy, and other environmentally responsible actions. In recognition of the fiftieth anniversary of Earth Day, the Global Earth Challenge was launched in twenty twenty, a global citizen science initiative to show how small acts of science can add up to global change. New and continuing challenges, including climate change, pollution, and loss of forests, wetlands, and other wildlife habitats, reinforce the need for adequate protections for the air we breathe, the water we drink, the land we live on, and the animals we live alongside. There is scientific agreement that climate change poses a serious threat to our planet and its inhabitants, and that urgent and unprecedented changes are needed to reduce its worst effects.

sections.title: Recent Legislation and Concerns
sections.text: Earth Day reaffirms our national responsibility in the global effort to combat climate change and protect our planet for all. In twenty twenty-one, Congress passed the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, which made foundational investments in clean drinking water, clean transportation, clean energy, resilient infrastructure, and pollution cleanup. In twenty twenty-two, Congress passed what is commonly called the Inflation Reduction Act of twenty twenty-two, the largest climate bill in American history and a transformative investment in our future. This bill includes unprecedented funding for clean energy, zero-emission vehicles, sustainable aviation, energy efficiency, climate-smart agriculture and forestry, and decarbonizing heavy industry, all while supporting and funding climate change mitigation and adaptation initiatives in those historically disadvantaged communities that are most vulnerable to the effects of climate change. These legislative actions have shown the capacity for climate investments to strengthen American industry, support resilient supply chains, build critical infrastructure, and generate economic opportunity. Efforts to repeal these landmark laws or undermine their implementation would have catastrophic consequences for America and the world as we face the increasing impacts of climate change.

sections.title: Current Concerns and Earth Day Mission
sections.text: Recent actions to dismantle Federal agencies, shut down critical programs, and illegally withhold funding approved by Congress will harm Americans across the country. The rollback of environmental regulations will pollute the air and contaminate the water of the United States, jeopardizing the health of Americans for years to come. The withdrawal from the Paris Agreement is a step toward giving up American leadership in the global effort to protect the environmental health of the planet. Earth Day encourages us to acknowledge the prevalence of environmental injustices that directly affect the health and well-being of individuals and communities across the country, and affirms our commitment to improving existing environmental injustices and preventing future injustices. Earth Day reminds us of the need to expand environmental and climate literacy not only to strengthen environmental and climate protections but also to accelerate development of green technologies and creation of green jobs that will benefit all. The mission and purpose of Earth Day remains relevant today because pollution, environmental degradation, and the climate crisis are issues that affect multiple generations. Earth Day reminds us that we must not pass a world beyond repair to our children.

sections.title: House Resolution Actions
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives urges the President to issue a proclamation recognizing Earth Day. The House encourages the American people to mark Earth Day by working to address environmental challenges large and small, from climate change to litter. The House encourages Americans to uplift indigenous environmental knowledge and stewardship practices, to educate their friends, neighbors, and elected representatives about the need for year-round action, and to otherwise honor the stewardship ethic for which Senator Gaylord Nelson stood. The House urges the United States Government to rejoin the Paris Agreement and prioritize other efforts to ensure that future generations inherit a livable, sustainable, and ecologically rich planet Earth. The House reaffirms that immediate action is needed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve environmental injustices in order to protect the Earth, the public health, and the overall well-being of all people and wildlife.
